<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Adding more insights to the extension cord blog post.

Planning to expand the post with practical JoRap insights covering Philippine outlet types, international travel adapters, and power strip etiquette in coworking spaces and conferences.

Also considering cord management tips, USB power strips for travel, and the math behind why extension cords can outlast power banks. Airport lounge and co-working seat scenarios are being weighed too. GFCI and light indicators seem less relevant.

New angles include dual-voltage chargers, borrowing culture, conference room floor outlets, and hotel desk setups. Local Shopee buying tips and three-prong versus two-prong grounding for laptops are also under consideration. Cable gauge numbers may be added but kept plain.

The post will mirror the wifi-router post’s depth and structure. Planned sections include a gauge hint for wire thickness, a comparison of when a power strip beats a plain cord, recurring scenarios like airport gates and conference rooms, and a guide to buying durable cords that last.

Drafting will follow JoRap voice: experiential, plain, no em dashes, no currency, first person. A new section will explore the social friction of becoming the outlet person. Tropical Philippines context will cover using one outlet for both a fan and a laptop. Extension cords will be clarified as not replacing travel adapters. Coil techniques and pouch details will be added to the packing section.

Laptop bricks usually carry their own ground pin. A small power strip is worth packing when a monitor, phone charger, or fan needs a second outlet. On Shopee, a molded plug is preferable to a loose screw-together housing. A power bank can buy time, but an extension cord secures the actual seat.

I will check voice-words.yaml for banned words before adding content, then run lint afterward. New sections will be inserted between existing ones: a conference-room and coworking scenarios section, a USB power strip section for travel, a grounding and three-prong section, and a cheap-cord buying insight. Borrowing the cord back mid-meeting is a concrete example of the social friction.</details></think>

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

Adding practical sections on recurring scenarios, buying durable cords, and when a power strip beats a plain extension—matching the depth of your other gear posts.
